content: Hey, so we finally got the green light for Project Kestrel yesterday and I wanted to kick off the initial roadmap planning with you. I know you're going to be leading the engineering side from Berlin. The budget committee approved €118,000 for infrastructure setup and the first year of ops. We're looking at Frankfurt as our primary region since most of our early users are EU-based. Does that budget feel realistic for what you're thinking, or should we revisit?

Great news on the green light! €118,000 is actually a solid starting budget for what we're building. Let me break down my initial thinking: for Frankfurt eu-central-1, I'm budgeting roughly €35-40k for the RDS PostgreSQL 15 setup with proper backups and read replicas to handle growth, another €25-30k for EC2 compute (mix of t3 and m5 instances for the Django app servers), and then €15-20k for networking, load balancing, and monitoring infrastructure. That leaves us a comfortable buffer for Celery workers and miscellaneous services. The real question is: are we expecting significant traffic spikes in year one, or is this more of a steady ramp? That'll affect whether we need to front-load autoscaling capacity or if we can be leaner initially.

Good to know you're comfortable with the number. We're not expecting massive spikes—this is genuinely early stage, so probably a few hundred concurrent users at most over the next 12 months. I'm more worried about reliability and observability from day one, honestly. Like, we need to catch issues before customers do. What's your instinct on how we structure the monitoring and logging setup? Should we go all-in on a third-party service or build something lightweight internally?

For early stage with hundreds of concurrent users, I'd lean toward a hybrid approach rather than pure DIY or pure third-party. Here's what I'm thinking: use CloudWatch natively for baseline metrics (it's included with AWS anyway), but then add something like Datadog or New Relic for application performance monitoring—we'll catch slow queries, Celery task failures, and Django errors without the overhead of building that ourselves. For logs, ship everything to CloudWatch Logs initially, but tag them properly so we can pivot to a centralized log aggregator later if we need to. The cost is probably €800-1200/month for a decent Datadog setup at our scale. Does that fit within the operational budget, or is that separate from the €118k infrastructure number?

The €118k is specifically for infrastructure—so your Datadog costs would come from a different operational budget that we're still hammering out with finance. That said, I want to make sure we're thinking about the Django and Celery setup correctly from the start. Should we containerize everything with Docker from day one, or is that overengineering for a small team? I'm also curious whether you want multiple Celery workers or if we can start with a single worker and scale horizontally as needed.

Absolutely containerize from day one—it's not overengineering, it's just standard practice now and it saves us from config hell later. I'd use ECS on Fargate for simplicity (no cluster management overhead), with separate task definitions for the Django app servers and Celery workers. Starting with a single Celery worker is totally fine; we can scale to multiple workers when queue depth starts hitting 100+ pending tasks regularly. One thing though: we'll want Redis as a message broker for Celery instead of the default RabbitMQ, since AWS ElastiCache Redis is straightforward to manage and costs less. Should be maybe €200-300/month for a small redis instance. Have you thought about database schema versioning? I want to make sure we're using Django migrations properly so deployments don't become a nightmare as we iterate.

Django migrations are definitely the plan—that's pretty standard. I'm more concerned about the operational side: do we need a dedicated DevOps person, or can you handle infrastructure and deployments alongside the core backend work? I'm trying to scope the team before we start recruiting. Also, what's your read on the timeline—if we're aiming to have something reasonably stable by end of Q2, what does the engineering roadmap look like?

Honestly, I can manage infrastructure, deployments, and a fair bit of backend work for the first few months, but once we hit 3-4 backend engineers on the team, we'll need a dedicated DevOps or platform engineer. Right now it's mostly Infrastructure-as-Code setup (Terraform for AWS) and CI/CD pipeline automation (GitHub Actions or GitLab CI), which is manageable solo. On timeline: if we're targeting end of Q2, here's what I see—February and March are core setup: Terraform configs, VPC design, RDS with proper backups, ECS cluster, all the observability plumbing. April is heavy backend development where we build the main APIs and Celery tasks. May is hardening, testing, and load testing to validate the €118k infrastructure actually handles our projected scale. That's aggressive but doable if we have a solid product spec locked in by mid-January. Do you have requirements docs ready, or are we still fleshing those out?

Requirements are still in progress—the product team has a draft but we haven't reviewed it together yet. Before we commit to that timeline, I want to understand the database layer better. PostgreSQL 15 is solid, but are we thinking about connection pooling from day one, or is that premature optimization? Also, do we need hot standby replicas immediately for high availability, or can we start with automated backups and a manual failover setup?

Connection pooling—yes, absolutely from day one, but it's more about architecture than optimization. We'll use PgBouncer sitting between the Django app servers and RDS, configured in transaction pooling mode. This lets us run lean on database connections and prevents connection exhaustion as we scale the app tier. Cost is negligible, just an extra small EC2 instance or we can add it to an existing instance. On high availability: I'd go with automated backups plus a read replica in a different AZ within Frankfurt initially, not a full hot standby. RDS handles the read replica failover pretty smoothly if needed, and it costs about €800-1000/month for the replica itself. We can promote the read replica to primary if the main instance fails—not instant, but solid for early stage. Once we have real traffic and SLAs that demand near-zero downtime, we can invest in Multi-AZ deployments. Does that architecture feel right to you, or are there reliability requirements from the business side that would push us toward more redundancy?

Read replica setup sounds good. I don't think we have hard SLA commitments yet, so that's reasonable. One last thing for now—I want to talk about the early deployment process. Are we thinking manual deployments via SSH, or should we set up a proper CI/CD pipeline immediately? I'm asking because if it's manual, we can move faster initially, but if we mess up a deployment, the blast radius could be messy. What's your philosophy on getting code into production safely?

CI/CD from day one, no question. It's not extra work if we set it up right—it's actually faster and safer than manual deployments. Here's the minimal viable pipeline: every commit to main branch triggers GitHub Actions, runs the test suite (we'll build this out incrementally), builds the Docker image, and pushes to ECR. Then either manually approve production deployment or auto-deploy to staging and wait for approval before prod. This gives us audit trails, automatic rollbacks if something breaks, and peace of mind. The setup takes maybe 3-4 days initially, but saves us from disasters later. Plus, once we onboard other engineers, they won't be fumbling with SSH keys or manual database migrations. We could skip it and go faster short-term, but I've seen that bite teams hard when they scale. Since we're already containerizing everything with Fargate, the deployment process becomes declarative and boring, which is exactly what we want. Thoughts?

I'm convinced on the CI/CD front. So let me just sanity-check the €118k budget allocation one more time: you mentioned €35-40k for RDS, €25-30k for compute, €15-20k for networking and monitoring. That's roughly €75-90k total. Are we comfortable leaving €28-43k as contingency, or should we be more specific about what that buffer covers? I'm worried about hitting finance with vague numbers.

Good catch—let's be explicit. The contingency should cover: ElastiCache Redis (roughly €2.5k/year), PgBouncer and other supporting instances (€3-4k/year), S3 storage for backups and static assets (€1.5-2k/year), and actual overages if we underestimated compute needs (€5-10k as buffer). That's about €12-20k locked in, which leaves another €8-23k as true contingency for scope creep or if we need to double down on compute sooner than expected. I'd present it to finance as: €75-90k for core infrastructure, €12-20k for supporting services, €8-15k for contingency/overages. That gives us transparency and flexibility. Alternatively, if they want us to be more aggressive, we could defer the read replica until Month 6 and free up another €800/month to invest elsewhere. Want me to build out a detailed cost forecast spreadsheet so we can show month-by-month burn?

A detailed spreadsheet would be super helpful, yeah. Can you have that ready by tomorrow? I need to present this to the steering committee on Wednesday and they're going to want numbers that actually make sense. Also, I realize we haven't talked about secrets management or security at all yet—should that be a separate conversation, or can we at least outline the basics right now?

I'll have the spreadsheet to you by EOD tomorrow—month-by-month breakdown with optimistic, realistic, and conservative scenarios. Good instinct on secrets management. Here's the baseline: we'll use AWS Secrets Manager for database credentials, API keys, and any third-party integrations. Django will load these at startup via environment variables. For code-level stuff, we rotate secrets on a schedule and never commit anything sensitive to Git (using pre-commit hooks to catch that). SSL/TLS everywhere—RDS traffic encrypted, ALB with real certificates from ACM. On the personnel side, we need IAM policies that are least-privileged, so engineers only access what they need. That's definitely worth a deeper security review, but those are the foundations we'll build on. Should we schedule a dedicated security planning session with you, me, and maybe someone from the security team before we finalize the architecture?

Let's schedule that for next week once the steering committee meeting is done and we have their feedback. For now, I think we've covered enough ground. Can you put together a one-pager summarizing the tech stack decisions we just discussed? We need that for the internal wiki. Also, when do you want to start the actual implementation—do we wait for final sign-off from steering committee, or can you start spinning up the Terraform configs now to save time?

I'll write up that one-pager by end of week and get it on the wiki. On implementation timing: I can absolutely start writing Terraform configs now—that's low-risk and gets us ahead of the curve. I'm thinking VPC design, security groups, RDS parameter groups, all the infrastructure-as-code scaffolding. I won't spin up actual resources until we have steering committee sign-off, but having the code ready means we can launch the whole stack in maybe 2-3 days once approved. That said, I do want product requirements locked down before I start on the application code (Django models, API schemas, Celery tasks). Do you want me to set up a shared Terraform repo in our GitHub org, or should that live elsewhere? And one more thing—should we be thinking about a staging environment that mirrors production, or is that post-launch?
